learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Greedy Algorithm, Loops, Time Complexity, Space Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine driving around a circular highway with fuel stations. At every station, you refuel before driving to the next station. Your goal is to determine the only station from which you can complete the entire loop without running out of fuel.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine driving around a circular highway with fuel stations. At every station, you refuel before driving to the next station. Your goal is to determine the only station from which you can complete the entire loop without running out of fuel.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this Greedy pattern whenever a failed starting point eliminates an entire range of future candidates.
learning.signals: Whenever failure at one position eliminates a whole range of future candidates, think of a Greedy Traversal.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: There are n gas stations arranged in a circular route. You are given two integer arrays gas and cost, where gas[i] is the amount of gas available at station i and cost[i] is the gas required to travel from station i to station (i + 1). Return the starting gas station's index if you can travel around the circuit once in the clockwise direction; otherwise, return -1. If a solution exists, it is guaranteed to be unique.

examples.explanation: Starting from station 3 allows completing the entire circuit.
Total gas is less than total cost, so completing the circuit is impossible.

edgeCases: Total gas less than total cost
Starting station is the last index
All stations have equal gas and cost

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Attempt to complete the circuit starting from every gas station until a valid one is found.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: If you cannot reach station B starting from station A, then every station between A and B also cannot be a valid starting point. Therefore, when your fuel becomes negative, you can safely skip all those stations and start searching from the next station.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: If you fail between stations A and B, none of the stations between A and B can be a valid starting point.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever failure at one position eliminates a whole range of future candidates, think of a Greedy Traversal.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Every station is visited exactly once. Whenever a starting station fails, all stations before the failure are eliminated from consideration, resulting in a linear-time greedy solution.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever failure at one position eliminates a whole range of future candidates, think of a Greedy Traversal.
commonWrongPaths: Not checking if total gas is less than total cost.
Resetting total gas instead of current fuel.
Confusing total fuel with current fuel.

hints: Compare total gas and total cost first.
Restart whenever the balance becomes negative.
commonMisconceptions: Not checking if total gas is less than total cost.
Resetting total gas instead of current fuel.
Confusing total fuel with current fuel.

pattern.name: Greedy Traversal
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever failure at one position eliminates a whole range of future candidates, think of a Greedy Traversal.

approaches.title: Try Every Starting Station
approaches.idea: Attempt to complete the circuit starting from every gas station until a valid one is found.
algorithm: Choose every station as the starting point.
approaches.dryRun:
Start from 0 → Fail
Start from 1 → Fail
Start from 3 → Success

code.java: Brute force simulation omitted because it tries every starting station.

approaches.title: Greedy Restart
approaches.idea: Maintain the current fuel balance. Whenever it becomes negative, discard all previous stations and restart from the next station.
algorithm: Compute total gas and total cost.
approaches.dryRun:
Fuel becomes negative at station 2.
Restart from station 3.

approaches.title: Greedy Traversal
approaches.idea: First verify that the total gas is at least the total travel cost. Then traverse the stations while maintaining the current fuel balance. Whenever the balance becomes negative, reset the balance to zero and choose the next station as the new candidate starting point.
approaches.intuition: Every station is visited exactly once. Whenever a starting station fails, all stations before the failure are eliminated from consideration, resulting in a linear-time greedy solution.
algorithm: After traversal, if totalGas < totalCost return -1.
approaches.dryRun:
gas=[1,2,3,4,5]
cost=[3,4,5,1,2]
Fuel=-2 → Restart at 1
Fuel=-2 → Restart at 2
Fuel=-2 → Restart at 3
Complete journey
Answer=3

visualization.description: Whenever fuel becomes negative, restart from the next station.

interviewFollowUps: Why can failed stations be skipped?
Can multiple valid starting stations exist?
How would you prove the greedy solution?
Can this problem be solved using prefix sums?
interviewTips: Mention why total gas must be checked.
State O(n) time and O(1) space.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: If you fail between stations A and B, none of the stations between A and B can be a valid starting point.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Check Total → Traverse → Restart on Negative Fuel.

importantPoints: Total gas must be at least total cost.
commonMistakes: Not checking if total gas is less than total cost.
Resetting total gas instead of current fuel.
Confusing total fuel with current fuel.
